ontology.foodex2_description: Onion powder — dehydrated and ground onion used as a seasoning
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for onion powder as a food ingredient; SNOMED CT coverage for spice and seasoning subcategories is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of onion powder is approximately 1 teaspoon (about 3 g), used as a seasoning in cooking.

nutrient_source.title: Onion powder — Singapore HPB Foodo / USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170931)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary macronutrient and mineral values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo (data_source: hpb, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and additional micronutrient values cross-referenced and supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for onion powder (FDC ID 170931). Values are consistent between both approved sources.

gi_source.title: Onion powder GI estimated from dehydrated onion / onion analogous food data; closest peer-reviewed reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for onion powder was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 32 is estimated from the closest analogous food: onion (raw/cooked), which has a reported GI of approximately 10–35 in peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008). Dehydration concentrates carbohydrates but the dominant carbohydrate fraction is fructooligosaccharides and fructose, which have low glycaemic impact. GI of 32 (low category) is a conservative defensible estimate. Glycemic load per 3 g serving = (32 × 2.37 g available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = 0.76, rounded to 0.7.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (73 mg/100g) and exceptionally high potassium (985 mg/100g) with a favourable Na:K ratio
hypertension.guidance: Onion powder is an excellent low-sodium, high-potassium seasoning for hypertensive patients; at a typical 1–5 g serving it contributes less than 4 mg sodium and approximately 10–49 mg potassium, supporting DASH dietary targets without raising blood pressure risk — however, patients must avoid onion salt or seasoning blends that contain added sodium.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High per-100g carbohydrate (79.1 g) is clinically irrelevant at gram-level seasoning use; low GI (~32), high dietary fibre (15.2 g/100g), and quercetin content support glycaemic control
type2_diabetes.guidance: At a typical serving of 1–3 g, onion powder contributes less than 2.4 g carbohydrate and has a negligible glycaemic load of under 1, making it safe for people with Type 2 Diabetes; its quercetin and fructooligosaccharide content may additionally support insulin sensitivity, but patients should avoid commercial onion powder blends containing added sugar or maltodextrin.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat (0.16 g/100g), zero trans fat, high potassium, high dietary fibre, and quercetin with documented cardioprotective anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Onion powder is a cardioprotective seasoning choice due to its negligible saturated fat, zero cholesterol, high potassium-to-sodium ratio, and quercetin content, which has been associated with reduced LDL oxidation and endothelial protection in peer-reviewed studies; it can be used freely as a salt substitute in heart-healthy cooking.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Rich in quercetin, a polyphenol that inhibits pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6) relevant to RA pathogenesis
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Onion powder is suitable for people with rheumatoid arthritis; its quercetin content provides anti-inflammatory benefit, and its negligible saturated fat and trans fat content avoids pro-inflammatory dietary load — use pure dehydrated onion powder without added salt to maximise benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Onion powder is a dehydrated and finely ground spice produced by slicing or chopping whole onions (Allium cepa), drying them at low temperature to remove approximately 94% of their moisture content, and then milling the dried flakes into a fine, pale-yellow to off-white free-flowing powder. The resulting product has a dry, slightly granular texture that dissolves readily when added to liquids or moist foods. Its flavour is intensely savoury and pungent — far more concentrated than fresh onion — with a sweet, slightly sulphurous undertone that mellows during cooking to produce a deep, rounded onion flavour without the sharp bite of raw alliums. Onion powder is used globally across virtually every culinary tradition as a seasoning in dry rubs, spice blends, marinades, soups, stews, sauces, gravies, and processed foods. It is a staple in American barbecue seasoning, South Asian masala blends, European bouillon preparations, and Southeast Asian spice pastes. Per 100 g, onion powder provides 341 kcal, 10.4 g protein, 1.0 g fat (of which only 0.16 g is saturated and 0.0 g is trans fat), 79.1 g carbohydrate, 15.2 g dietary fibre, 6.6 g sugar, 73 mg sodium, 985 mg potassium, 384 mg calcium, 3.9 mg iron, and 349 mg phosphorus. Despite the high per-100g carbohydrate figure, typical culinary use is 1–5 g per dish, making the actual carbohydrate and caloric contribution negligible in practice. Onion powder has a low estimated glycaemic index of approximately 32 and a glycaemic load under 1 per standard serving. It is exceptionally rich in quercetin, a flavonoid polyphenol with well-documented anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and cardioprotective properties that inhibit pro-inflammatory cytokines including TNF-α and IL-6. The outstanding potassium-to-sodium ratio makes it an ideal DASH-diet-aligned seasoning for patients managing hypertension. Clinically, it is suitable for people with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis when used as a pure dehydrated onion product without added salt, sugar, or maltodextrin. It is naturally vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher, and serves as a practical flavour-enhancing alternative to salt in therapeutic diets.
common_user_queries: Is onion powder high in sodium?
What is the difference between onion powder and onion salt?
How many calories in onion powder?
Is onion powder good for blood pressure?
Is onion powder vegan and gluten-free?
What nutrients are in onion powder?
Can I use onion powder instead of fresh onion?

confidence_reason: Primary nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95, cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170931) for onion powder. Values are consistent between both approved sources. GI is a defensible analogous estimate (low category, ~32) based on peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care), which is the best available evidence for this food.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (0.16 g/100g), monounsaturated fat (0.14 g/100g), polyunsaturated fat (0.33 g/100g), and trans fat (0.0 g/100g) sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 170931 to supplement HPB data. Trans fat is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy. 2. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, and selenium_mcg set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for onion powder and are not consistently reported in USDA SR Legacy for this item; data unavailability confirmed by consulting both HPB Foodo and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 170931 as of 2026-04-16. 3. vitamin_b12_mcg (0.0) and vitamin_d_mcg (0.0) are naturally zero: onion powder is a plant-derived food that inherently contains no vitamin B12 or vitamin D; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 170931. 4. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: naturally zero as onion powder is a plant-derived food containing no cholesterol; confirmed zero per HPB Foodo and USDA SR Legacy. 5. GI of 32 is an analogous estimate based on onion (raw/cooked) GI values from Atkinson et al. (2008) International Tables of GI and GL Values, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283; no direct GI measurement for onion powder exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026-04-16. 6. Glycaemic load per 3 g serving calculated as (32 × 2.37 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 0.76, rounded to 0.7. 7. The dietary tag 'wholegrain' from the input has been removed as onion powder is a dehydrated vegetable product, not a grain; 'low_sodium' and 'high_fibre' tags are retained as accurate. 8. local_names fields are all null: onion powder is a global generic seasoning ingredient with no cuisine-specific origin requiring mandatory local-language naming under G6 rules.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index (32), gi_category ('low'), glycemic_load_per_serving (0.7), and gi_source all present. GI is an analogous estimate from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care), a peer-reviewed journal, which is an approved GI source. Estimation basis fully documented.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn URI provided (FOODON_03301710, onion powder — exact match). FoodEx2 code A00LC provided for onion powder seasoning. SNOMED CT is null with documented gap note. No codes invented.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is approximately 1,650 characters, well above the 200-character minimum. It explicitly covers: (1) preparation method — slicing, low-temperature drying, milling; (2) sensory profile — dry granular texture, intensely savoury and pungent flavour, sweet sulphurous undertone that mellows during cooking; (3) cultural context — use across American barbecue, South Asian masala, European bouillon, Southeast Asian spice pastes; (4) clinical relevance — suitability for Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, rheumatoid arthritis with specific quercetin mechanism; (5) key nutrients — full macro and micronutrient profile cited. All five Q6 dimensions are substantively addressed.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (low concern), Type 2 Diabetes (low concern, machine key type2_diabetes, human name 'Type 2 Diabetes'), Cardiovascular Disease (low concern). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and a specific clinical guidance sentence.
